Copper-coloured rays of the Sun bring about a general's
death; yellow and rosy ones, his misery (or danger from diseases);
green ones, the destruction of cattle and crops; smoky ones, the
ruin of cows; madder-coloured rays cause trouble- through clash of
arms and fires; tawny ones, sltormx rain; ash-cdloured ones, drought;
and spotted and greyish ori^s, slight rain (or emaciation).
is another reading for The commentator
interprets as sligktness of rain by supplying the word «T^:. Had
it been the author's intention, he would have plainly used the
expression without harming the metre. Hence I feel that the
expression should mean general debility or emaciation of people."
In this section the author shows his familiarity with the use of
